coredata.dc:description: Graphene has distinctive mechanical, electronic, and optical properties, which researchers have applied to develop innovative electronic materials including transparent conductors and ultrafast transistors. Lately, the understanding of various chemical properties of graphene has expedited its application in high-performance devices that generate and store energy. Graphene is now increasing its terrain outside electronic and chemical applications toward biomedical areas such as precise bio sensing through graphene-quenched fluorescence, graphene-enhanced cell differentiation and growth, and graphene-assisted laser desorption/ionization for mass spectrometry. In this Account, we evaluate recent efforts to apply graphene and graphene oxides (GO) to biomedical research and a few different approaches to prepare graphene materials designed for biomedical applications and a brief perspective on their future applications. Because of its outstanding aqueous processability, amphiphilicity, surface functionalizability, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and fluorescence quenching ability, GO chemically exfoliated from oxidized graphite is considered a promising material for biological applications. In addition, the hydrophobicity and flexibility of large-area graphene synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) allow this material to play an important role in cell growth and differentiation. Graphene is considered to be an encouraging and smart candidate for numerous biomedical applications such as NIR-responsive cancer therapy and fluorescence bio-imaging and drug delivery. To that end, suitable preparation and unique approaches to utilize graphene-based materials such as graphene oxides (GOs), reduced graphene oxides (rGOs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in biology and medical science are gaining growing interest.

head.ce:title: Bioapplications of graphene constructed functional nanomaterials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene has distinctive mechanical, electronic, and optical properties, which researchers have applied to develop innovative electronic materials including transparent conductors and ultrafast transistors. Lately, the understanding of various chemical properties of graphene has expedited its application in high-performance devices that generate and store energy. Graphene is now increasing its terrain outside electronic and chemical applications toward biomedical areas such as precise bio sensing through graphene-quenched fluorescence, graphene-enhanced cell differentiation and growth, and graphene-assisted laser desorption/ionization for mass spectrometry. In this Account, we evaluate recent efforts to apply graphene and graphene oxides (GO) to biomedical research and a few different approaches to prepare graphene materials designed for biomedical applications and a brief perspective on their future applications. Because of its outstanding aqueous processability, amphiphilicity, surface functionalizability, surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and fluorescence quenching ability, GO chemically exfoliated from oxidized graphite is considered a promising material for biological applications. In addition, the hydrophobicity and flexibility of large-area graphene synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) allow this material to play an important role in cell growth and differentiation. Graphene is considered to be an encouraging and smart candidate for numerous biomedical applications such as NIR-responsive cancer therapy and fluorescence bio-imaging and drug delivery. To that end, suitable preparation and unique approaches to utilize graphene-based materials such as graphene oxides (GOs), reduced graphene oxides (rGOs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in biology and medical science are gaining growing interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Ever since its first serendipitous, nevertheless historical discovery by British scientists in 2004[1], graphene has engrossed momentous attention of researchers from all fields of science for exploiting many of its unique properties. One of the key research concentrations have been replacing indium tin oxide (ITO) with large-scale, high-quality chemical vapor deposition (CVD) graphene for macroscopic applications such as flexible thin films for transparent electrodes. Further studies in the fields of electronics, physics, and materials science have also been broadly explored [2]. In recent times, given a growing consensus on graphene's environmentally-friendly features, researchers have deliberated engaging graphene in other branches of science such as biology and medicine. Researchers have predominantly concentrated on utilizing the capability of graphene oxides (GOs) to quench fluorescence and the readiness of their functional groups for molecular conjugation for several optical bio-sensing studies. In 2009, Lu et al. efficaciously detected fluorophore-labeled DNA on/off the basal plane of GOs [3]. This work was followed by spotting other small molecules such as phosphate containing metabolites, protein kinases, trypsin and neurotransmitters with appropriate surface modulations [4]. A latest report by Mei et al. demonstrated that logically designed GO gates could discriminate Fe3+ and Fe2+ in living cells by take advantage of the difference in fluorescence quenching [5]. Various researchers dedicated special courtesy to uncommon characteristics of stem cell growth and differentiation on graphene film substrates, which could probably open new venues in stem cell engineering [6]. Besides, extraordinary optical absorbance of GOs, reduced graphene oxides (rGOs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the near-infrared (NIR) region expedite selective photothermal/photodynamic applications, making graphene a favorable multipurpose therapeutic tool. Especially, researchers have demonstrated that malignant tumor cells can be directly ablated using NIR-responsive photothermal therapy [7]. Graphene centred materials have also been engaged to assist in several photodynamic therapies, certain display the chance of combining graphene-based photodynamic agents with either photothermal therapies or chemotherapies [8]. Further studies have employed graphene hyperthermia as an external prompt for efficient and controlled gene/drug delivery either by disrupting an endosome or a drug containing matrix [9]. Graphene materials comprising of exclusively carbon are acknowledged to be non-toxic; still, it is a matter of grave concern to know how carbon derivatives like graphene decay in a biological system and how long it takes to excrete them from the biological system [10]. Though, throughout fabrication, graphene or sources of graphene commonly experience numerous chemical treatment progressions for functionalization, comprising doping with metals, oxidation introduction of functional groups and also reduction [11]. This designates that some of the graphene derivatives deliberated for bio-applications contain metals and/or impurities other than carbon. For example, graphene quantum dots contain around 10%–40% oxygen and 60% carbon. The presence of excess oxygen is one of the standard features that augments the solubility of graphene quantum dots and impart optical properties to them. Furthermore, different graphene derivatives have different chemical properties with different functionalities and applications, thus they exert different toxicities [12]. Yet very inadequate studies have recently been conducted to detect the mechanisms of toxicology of graphene derivatives, especially of GO due to oxidative stress; no universal mechanism has been established yet [13].Table 1

Besides the studies deliberated above, dispersed graphene derivatives are recognized to demonstrate exceptional characteristics suitable for multipurpose imaging applications. Even though technical innovations in science have generated different ways for appreciating diverse cellular/subcellular proceedings on highly sophisticated levels, the real time imaging with sufficiently high temporal and spatial resolutions still remains reasonably thought-provoking in many aspects. Simultaneously developing effective and stable fluorescent probes has been and remains to be one of the most important tasks in fluorescence bioimaging. Similarly, other imaging tools such as Raman spectroscopy, which exploits scattered light derived from the vibrational excitation mode of molecules, necessitates adequate imaging agents for creating clear and sharp signals. By the virtue of the exclusive properties of graphene-based materials, they are becoming gradually spotlighted as versatile imaging tools for assisting in both optical and nonoptical imaging studies. Our goal here is to review the status of current research on graphene-based imaging studies and drug/gene delivery and discuss the perspectives for future applications (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene derivatives and their optical properties

ce:section.ce:para: Basic graphene sheets with enormously huge sp2 domains are not photoluminescent due to their zero band gap energy. In order to achieve photoluminescent graphene, numerous approaches have been advocated for generating the band gap by modifying p electronic structures. Fabricating minor particles by oxidative cutting of graphene, producing tiny sp2 fields by plasma engraving or amending indigenous electronic structures by chemical modifications all efficaciously manipulate the electronic structure of graphene sheets [14]. It has been described systematically, that mechanisms of photoluminescence in graphene derivatives are not wholly unstated. Even though the thorough mechanisms of photoluminescence are pondered upon, experimental observations and theoretical calculations conjecture two likely mechanisms. One narrates to the band-gap transition, as it commonly occurs in various semiconductor systems, whereas the former relates to the electron–hole recombination pertinent to the presence of defects. Eda et al. established that fractional reduction of GOs boosts blue photoluminescence [15]. In that study, early (∼3 min) exposure of GOs to hydrazine (N2H4) vapor enhanced photoluminescence at around 390 nm, despite the fact additional exposure reasonably reduced the intensity. Sp [2] domains energy gaps rely on the domain size as defined by the time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT), the energy of sp2 domain made up of 20 aromatic rings yields blue photoluminescence above 2 eV (Fig. 2 a). Theoretical calculations and experimental measurements specify that a minor reduction of GOs breeds nanosized sp2 domains in GOs, giving rise to blue photoluminescence, and effects supplementary reduction by amalgamation of the sp2 domains, thereby reduces the photoluminescence intensity. Also a sequence of studies established that the chemical modifications of graphene designate that photoluminescence is not only associated to the sp2 domain sizes, rather it is also affected by the variations in the indigenous electronic arrangements that are brought by defects. Simultaneous extraction of GQDs from GO sheets and modification of GQD edges by primary amines was demonstrated by Tetsuka et al. [16]. Amino-hydrothermal treatment in ammonia solution at low temperature effectually cut out the small sp2 domains entrenched in the GO sheets, and simultaneously primary amines bonded to the GQDs edges by ring opening amination of epoxides. The photoluminescence wavelengths of GQDs are blue-shifted with steadily declining reaction temperature, inferring that the higher amine density shifts the photoluminescence energy. Density-functional theory (DFT) and TD-DFT calculations were used to perform theoretical studies, which exposed that various edge modification tactics – heteroatom doping, conjugation and even defects vintage the anticipated photoluminescence tuning (Fig. 2b) [18]. Numerous reports measured the quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized GOs, and the reported QYs of GOs range from 0.02% to 70.3% [19]. Generally the synthesized GOs display very low QY and occasionally even impossible to measure, by introducing modest chemical reactions this can be improved. Minor reduction of GOs with hydrazine may perhaps freely yield a number of small sp2 clusters in GOs, subsequently the GOs would display blue photoluminescence [19b]. Optical properties of GOs can be improved by functionalization with organic molecules demonstrated. Amination of GOs by several alkylamines can surge the QY of GOs as confirmed by. Q. Mei et al. [19d]. The bases of non-radiative electron–hole recombination sites (epoxide and carboxide) are covalently bonded and passivated by alkylamines throughout reaction, aggregate the quantum yield from 0.02% to as high as 13%.

Graphene's Raman spectroscopy has been thoroughly studied and has turned out to be a standard tool for characterizing graphene. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been engaged eventually in bioimaging. CNTs, Distinguishing vibrational modes, the radial breathing mode and the G band are improved by means of electronic transition. Nevertheless, as the electronic structure of CNTs rely on the chirality and diameter, the as-synthesized CNTs attained via chemical vapor deposition (CVD) approaches show uneven properties. Graphene parades no chirality requirement in Raman scattering unlike CNTs, also the electronic structure of graphene has a small band gap, permitting a extensive range of photons (visible to NIR) to be exploited in Raman spectroscopy.

Graphene's Raman spectra exhibits few distinctive features, which are typically characteristic vibrational modes in the 1000 cm−1 to 3000 cm−1 range: the D peak, the G peak, and the 2D peak [20]. The D peak at 1350 cm−1 relates to the breathing mode, analogous to the one in CNTs however with higher frequency. The intensity of D peak is set on by the density of defects, for the reason that D peak is activated as long as defects are present. The 2D peak is every so often referred as G' peak, since it's positioned at around twice the frequency of the D peak. However defects present give rise to the D peak, due to the conservation momentum between two phonons in the course of scattering, the 2D peak is all the time observed in the spectrum. The G peak relates to the E2g symmetry phonon modes, which reveal the in-plane motion of the carbon atoms. Since the dispersed graphene, for example GOs and functionalized GOs, the inherent defects and uneven structure vintage intense and broadened D and G peaks, and an attenuated 2D peak [21]. By taking the advantage of C13 isotopes these peaks can be shifted, which support in revealing the mechanism of graphene synthesis and aiding multicolor imaging in the case of CNTs. Amid these, for bio-imaging the G band is frequently nominated to specify the amount of graphene derivatives as the intensity of the 2D peak is low in oxidized graphene derivatives.

The intensity of Raman scattering is vital for practical imaging applications. Despite any treatment graphene derivatives commonly demonstrate adequate Raman scattering intensity; which can be enhanced by employing additional metal nanoparticles. Schedin et al., performed an organized study to designate that the array of Au nanoparticles augments both the G peak and the 2D peak intensities [22]. Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) effect and dipole effect of metal nanoparticles causes the augmentation of graphene peaks. Raman signals of graphene are effectively increased by the commonly used noble metals for surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), gold and silver. These noble metal nanoparticles can be freely pooled with or straight away grown on GOs to augment the characteristic peaks in graphene as verified by the studies of Sun et al. [23]. Au and Ag nanoparticles with 10–30 nm size, the G peak displayed 4-fold augmentation in the case of graphene–Au, and 13-fold increase for graphene–Ag (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optics centred imaging and non optic centred imaging

ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent probe is supposed to fulfil few conditions for practical fluorescence imaging studies [25]. Notably, it has to be freely excitable with sufficiently high quantum yield. Fluorescence intensity and quantum yield are vital for curtailing fluorescent probe-grounded toxicity and radiation impairment by the incident laser light even though improving the fluorescence emission. Also the probe has to be amply resilient for upholding the original properties over long-standing seizure in biological fluids without blinking and photo bleaching. A fluorophore must not parade substantial cytotoxicity and it is appropriate to have functional groups accessible for conjugation with other molecules to successfully target exact objects of concern. During the course of such advances, several innovative contenders, for example green fluorescent proteins (GFPs) and inorganic quantum dots were planned and projected to be the ‘ideal’ fluorescent probes [26]. On the other hand, the widespread applicability of these probes is restricted owing to certain drawbacks such as photo bleaching and considerable cytotoxicity. Graphene derivatives have been proposed as an innovative class of fluorescent probes lately by researchers for biomedical imaging owing to their extraordinary characteristics. Photoluminescent GQDs have been engaged for a number of fluorescence imaging applications. By captivating the benefit of photos table, non-toxic and easily conjugatable GQDs researchers used them for multipurpose applications which comprise in situ drug delivery imaging.

Various drug delivery schemes and straight photothermal ablation of tumor cells have been complemented by numerous graphene-based platforms. Even though several of these studies performed in situ imaging of drug delivery/therapy, utmost of these studies exploited coated inorganic quantum dots and other fluorescence molecules conjugated to graphene-based materials to envisage the occurrences. Nahain et al., in 2013 revealed two graphene-established anti-cancer drug delivery approaches using rGOs and GQDs [27]. Spiropyran was also attached as a photochromic dye to give rise to a graphene-based fluorescent nanocomposite, in case of the rGO–hyaluronic acid (HA) conjugate system (avg. size D200 nm) [27a]. It's important to mention that these authors repeated related experiments devoid of attaching supplementary fluorescent materials. As a substitute, they made use of the intrinsic fluorescence of GQDs with an average size of 20 nm to authorize proficient targeting of GQD–HA to the anticipated receptors [27b]. By procurement fluorescence images from the tumor tissue via both in vitro and in vivo observations confirmed the successful delivery of the GQD–HA conjugate to overexpressed CD44 receptors (Fig. 4 ). Afterwards anti-cancer treatment was focussed by discharging doxorubicin in somewhat acidic conditions, which was encumbered onto the basal plane of GQDs. Although formerly studied graphene grounded therapy/imaging applications involved other fluorescent molecules, researchers venture to use the luminescence of GQDs for in situ therapy monitoring. Ge et al., In 2014 amalgamated a small number of nano-meter scale GQDs in exceedingly competent photodynamic cancer therapy with concurrent fluorescence imaging [28]. This study comprised the efficacious synthesis of GQDs by researchers with a broad absorption spectrum and strong deep-red emission peaking at 680 nm. The authors evidently established that GQDs can be deliberated as encouraging PDT agents, with a higher singlet oxygen quantum yield, photo- and pH-stability and even concurrent fluorescent imaging via both in vitro and in vivo experiments.

Despite the fact that record number of researchers have concentrated on taking advantage of GQDs' fluorescence for checking in situ drug delivery to endorse efficacious targeting, Zheng et al. established that GQDs can be employed as universal fluorophores that may perhaps reveal certain imperative biological function's (Fig. 5 ) [29]. Precise marking and vibrant tracking of insulin receptors were accomplished in this study via GQDs fluorescence of internalized and recycled insulin receptors in adipocytes. The specific functions of some relative proteins were tried to be established by the authors. By means of dynamically tracking the insulin receptors, the authors realized that the internalization and recycling of insulin receptors are antagonistically controlled by two distinct proteins: (1) apelin, which advances the insulin sensitivity, and (2) TNF a, which augments the insulin resistance. Even though this study single-handedly did not primarily modify the therapeutic tactics to diabetes cure, revealing important cellular/subcellular functions shown by employing the GQDs fluorescence would be fruitful for numerous future biomedical studies.

The ultimate common bio-imaging technique is fluorescence microscopy, but then again it is accompanied by certain shortcomings such as high excitation energy, photo-bleaching, and broad excitation/emission peak widths. While as in comparison, Raman spectroscopy employs scattered light resulting from molecular vibrational excitation modes. As a consequence, the energy of photon is not a prerequisite to match the electronic excitation energy, and lower energy of incident laser light can be castoff for evaluating the biological samples devoid of causing substantial damage. Moreover, reduced photo-bleaching and narrow peak width produce extra stable and multiplex observations [30]. On the other hand, the low efficiency of Raman scattering impedes it from fetching a universal imaging technique. One of the innovations is introduction of high-resolution EM-CCDs, but this is economically inauspicious. State-of-the-art, encouraging Raman imaging methods have been technologically advanced for bio-imaging applications, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) which elucidated the efficiency issue to some degree [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface-enhanced raman scattering (SERS) imaging by metal particle bedizenment
ce:section.ce:para: Contrasting to the small organic molecules, GOs display inherently strong D and G peaks deprived of any augmentations. By depositing metal nanoparticles graphene Raman peaks can be additionally boosted as we deliberated in the previous section. Direct growth of nanoparticles on hydrophilic GOs has been reported by numerous studies. Explicitly, gold nanoparticles and silver nanoparticles were ornamented on GOs by employing citrate [24], PVP [32], and DMF [33] as reducing agents., Synthesized nanoparticles could perhaps also be enthusiastically combined with GOs [34]in addition to the direct growth. As grown Au–graphene oxide composite employed by Liu et al., demonstrated extraordinarily improved D and G peaks under 632.8 nm laser illuminations [35]. Prolonged acquisition time and strong irradiation power tempted irradiation mutilation to cells can be inhibited with the exceedingly sensitive SERS effect of Au on the GOs surface. AuCl− 4 reduction induced by citrate yields an average of 20 nm Au nanoparticles on GOs, which remain generally under 400 nm in size. HeLa 229 cells incubated with Au nanoparticle–ornamented GO composites (Au–GO) divulged the cellular uptake mechanisms of Au–GO by Raman imaging. In disparity to the incubation at 37 °C, the intracellular Raman signals of Au–GO composites could not perceived upon incubation at 4 °C. The outcome advocates that the cell internalization of Au–GO composites happens due to ATP-dependent endocytosis.

In recent times gold nanoparticles solidly enfolded inside a nanosize GOs (NGO) as Raman imaging probes for a drug delivery system was reported by Ma et al. [36]. HAuCl4 and NGO solution were mixed, sonicated and reduced by means of a mild reducing agent, NaBH4. Using this procedure gave rise to NGO-encapsulated Au nanoparticles (Au@NGOs) with an average size of about 100 nm. The D and G peaks of Au@NGOs were approximately one fold of magnitude robust compared to those of the NGO, enlightening sensitive imaging of internalized Au@NGOs in HeLa cells (Fig. 6 ). In addition to the passive targeting of GO-centred nanostructures, an SERS-boosted imaging probe can be castoff to energetically target cancer cells by coupling folic acid (FA) with silver nanoparticle–decorated GOs, which is alike to the work described by Liu et al. [37], in 2013.

MRI is a precisely potent instrument for imaging the nervous system, cardiac system, and tumors [38]. It displays environmental-dependency for the lattice–spin (longitudinal) relaxation time T1 and spin–spin (transverse) relaxation time T2 of water proton's magnetic moment [39]. For that reason, under norms, MRI can be employed for distinguishing pathological tissues from healthy tissues in a non-invasive modus. So far, nearly, certain diseases do not practically produce distinctive relaxation times, so the ensuing tissue is inaccessible to MRI imaging. MRI contrast agents have to be introduced for augmenting the relaxation time difference, so as to diagnose these inaccessible pathological tissues. Recurrently employed and commercialized MRI contrasts are paramagnetic metal ion complexes and paramagnetic nanoparticles [40]. Due to lack of intrinsic paramagnetism, graphene on its own cannot oblige as an MRI contrast agent. Still, owing to the high density of oxygenated functional groups and cavities, GOs can be employed to hold the drugs with conventional MRI contrasts, and their readily functionalized edges permit the targeting ability concurrently. Moreover, attaching the MRI contrasts on GOs may perhaps alleviate the toxicity of heavy metal ion contrast agents because of decreased release rate. As a consequence, GOs can be engaged as a efficient platform for developing innovative multifunctional MR imaging agents. The schematic representation of Go based Gd compsites for MRI are shown in Fig. 7 .

Paramagnetic metals ions of gadolinium (Gd), manganese (Mn), and iron (Fe) possess high magnetic moments. As a consequence, spin–lattice relaxation takes place proficiently as soon as water molecules are coordinated on these ions. Since reduced net magnetization with time can accord to the T1 contrast, metal ions possessing high magnetic moments can be employed as T1 contrast agents.131 On the other hand, exceedingly paramagnetic metal ions are in general toxic due to the non-selective coordination with biomolecules. As a result, chelated forms are mostly employed as contrast agents [41]. Numerous oxygenated functional groups and cavities on graphene can be eagerly synchronized with the metal ions by chelation or entombing the ions between graphene layers. Kanakia et al. annexed Mn2+ ions in the dextran-coated graphene nanoplatelets (GNP-Dex) for T1-weighted MRI contrasts [42]. The annexed Mn2+ ions remained stable in GNP-Dex nanoparticles at the physiological temperature, as a consequence may well be used as clinical MRI contrasts. For each manganese ion concentration and the obtained slope was r1 = 92.2 mM−1 s−1 determined the relaxation rate. Moreover, improved T1-weighted phantom image due to the high r1 relaxivity recommends that Mn2+-intercalated GNP-Dex can assist as a good MRI contrast.

Nanoparticle-based MRI contrast agents commercially accessible are commonly built on magnetic iron oxides for instance the super paramagnetic iron oxide (SPIO) [43]. Super-paramagnetic clusters relaxation mechanism varies from that of in the paramagnetic ions. Due to the presence of a huge magnetic field around a paramagnetic nanoparticle, this prompts dephasing of water molecule spins near the nanoparticle, giving rise to the transverse relaxation T2 contrast. Since the nanoparticles can be unswervingly grown on graphene or capped ligands can be linked with graphene, paramagnetic nanoparticles can be straightforwardly pooled for generating graphene founded T2 contrast agents.

Chen et al., in 2011 first endeavoured to use a graphene-based T2 contrast agent [44]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles treated with DMSA were synthesized and covalently bonded with aminodextran (AMD), and then followed by EDC coupling with GOs to produce a Fe3O4–GO composite. The average size of Fe3O4–GO was 174.4 nm, realized by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The observed saturated magnetizations of the materials were 14, 11.5, 7.3 emu g−1 for DMSA–Fe3O4, AMD–Fe3O4, and GO–Fe3O4. The T2 relaxation rate as a function of Fe concentration of Fe3O4–GO was higher (r2 = 76 mM−1 s−1) than the others (r2 (DMSA–Fe3O4) = 24 mM−1 s−1, r2 (AMD–Fe3O4) = 21 mM−1 s−1), demonstrating that accumulation of magnetic particles on GOs augments the T2 contrast. Shi et al. testified iron oxide nanoparticle (IONPs) ornamented GOs for the MRI contrast agent [45]. The adsorbed IONPs on the surface of GO, were ensued by Au nanoparticle (AuNP) growth and PEG functionalization to create IONPs and AuNPs decorated GO complex (IONP–GO–Au), with average size drop between 200 and 600 nm. The acquired graphene-based material exhibited robust paramagnetic physiognomies originated from IONPs, and T2-weighted image evidently distinguished the tumor region (Fig. 8 ). Extra categories of paramagnetic nanoparticles may perhaps correspondingly be combined via using analogous method. Manganese ferrite (MnFe2O4) nanoparticle–GO composite produced T2 relaxation (r2 = 256.2 Fe mM−1 s-1) [46], and needle-shaped β-FeOOH nanorods displayed the peak T2 relaxation (r2 = 303.82 Fe mM−1 s−1) when pooled with GO[47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multifunctional MRI agents built on graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Recently theranostic multifunctional materials ought to attract a number of researchers' interests for the reason that efficient therapy can be accomplished by coalescing imaging, targeting and curing advantageously. Abundant functionality topographies permit potentially high loading capacity of drugs, and low toxicity renders them encouraging multifunctional platform candidates in the case of graphene derivatives. Wang et al. testified graphene-based multifunctional probes by coalescing magnetic graphene and mesophorous silica nanosheets [48]. Fe3O4 nanoparticle–ornamented graphene was coated with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), followed by coupling with interleukin-13-based peptide (IP) and doxorubicin (DOX). Graphene centred silica probes average size was 200 nm, besides the size of grown Fe3O4 nanoparticles was 4–15 nm. T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging of intravenously injected PEG conjugated nanocarrier (MGMSP) and supplementary IP conjugated nanocarrier (MGMSPI) validated that the targeting capability was instigated by IP. The DOX loading capacity of the IP-conjugated magnetic graphene mesophorous silicate (MGMSPID) touched 0.95 mg mg−1 with 43.19% loading efficiency, and in vitro DOX release by photothermal heating exposed that loaded DOX may perhaps be without difficulty desorbed with high concentration of hydrogen ions at high temperature owing to the enfeebled electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions. In the same way, one more type of anticancer drug, 5-FU, may perhaps be loaded on the Fe3O4 nanoparticle–GO composite [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene constructed Nanomaterial's for therapeutic agent delivery
Graphene constructed Nanomaterial's for drug delivery agents
ce:section.ce:para: Ever since their discovery as a bio-safe material, graphene has been castoff as a carrier molecule in drug delivery research [50]. The outsized precise surface area of graphene augments chances for multi-drug delivery to the target site from locate of administration. Polymeric alteration and conjugation approaches correspondingly augment biocompatibility and circulation times in vivo [51]. In the last couple of years numerous studies have been carried on the delivery of anticancer drugs, genes and peptides via graphene derivatives [52]. The p–p stacking can be employed for loading many hydrophobic drugs, such as doxorubicin and docetaxel, with antibodies for the selective killing of cancer cells through modest physisorption. Due to its small size, inherent optical properties, huge specific surface area, low cost and beneficial non-covalent interactions with aromatic drug molecules, graphene is an encouraging novel material for drug delivery via nano-carrier tactics. The huge specific surface area, p–p stacking and electrostatic or hydrophobic interactions of graphene might perhaps contribute in high drug loading of below par soluble drugs devoid of compromising potency or efficiency.

PEGylated GO loaded doxorubicin via p–p interactions demonstrate encouraging instantaneous release of DOX from PEGylated GO at a precise location adjoining an external triggering by GSH stated by Joo and his group [52b]. GO loaded with doxorubicin parades higher drug release at pH 5.3 owing to the abridged interaction between DOX and the drug carrier was stated by one more research group [52c]. GO loaded with DOX demonstrates improved cellular toxicity and encouraging tumor growth inhibition, with just about 66%–91% cell death52d-f. Additional chemotherapy drugs, for instance paclitaxel and methotrexate loaded on GO via p–p stacking and amide bonds, demonstrated astonishing cancerous effect on lung cancer and breast cancer, which led to the reticence of approximately 66%–90% tumor growth [52g,53]. As soon as ibuprofen, which is castoff as an NSAID, was conjugated with chitosan functionalized GO by way of amide linkages, the functionalized GO displayed elevated (20%) biocompatibility than GO sheets for CEM & MCF-7 cell lines [54]. A second generation photosensitizer chlorin e6 (Ce6) when loaded on GO lead to its elevated build-up in tumor cells, resulting a higher photodynamic efficacy upon irradiation [55]. Nano GO is one more vital material for the drug delivery research domain. For the delivery of water insoluble aromatic anticancer drugs into cells nanographene oxide (NGO) is employed as a unique and competent nanocarrier. Nano GO was first conjugated with PEG to achieve PEG–NGO in this methodology. Doxorubicin (DOX) and camptothecin (CPT) analog, SN38 were conjugated by non-covalent p–p interactions additionally. pH dependent drug release and noticeable cytotoxicity levels in HCT-116 cell lines which was 1000 fold potential than CPT [56] was displayed by these complexes. Near infrared (NIR) radiation, acidic pH and elevated intracellular concentrations of GSH preferred intracellular cytosolic delivery of DOX was testified by Kim et al. PEG and branched polyethylenimine (BPEI)-functionalized rGO (PEG–BPEI-rGO) nano-carriers treated cells when exposed to near-IR irradiation stimulated endosomal disruption and ensuing DOX release, which prompted cellular toxicity [57]. Conjugated graphene derivatives with biopolymers, for instance gelatin and polyethylene glycol, have been employed as functionalizing agents for drug delivery applications. Reduction of graphene is not only favoured by gelatin and polyethylene glycol but they also functionalized GNS, enabling the loading of DOX onto GNS. Through endocytosis the GNS–DOX complex also demonstrated extraordinary toxicity towards U251, 1800 and A-5RT3 cells [58]. Poly NIPAM and additional polymers ought to be castoff with graphene for loading numerous drugs such as camptothecin, methotexate [59], and 5-fluorouracil [60]. Weaver et al. [61] established that deposition of GO inside a conducting polymer scaffold (Fig. 9 ). The nanocomposite when loaded with an anti-inflammatory molecule, dexamethasone, it displayed worthy electrical properties. Consequently, this nanocomposite was competent to release the drug in a linear profile by electronic stimulation. GO can be loaded with doxorubicin, a extensively employed cancer drug functionalized with antibody for selective killing of cancer cells in vitro was testified by Sun et al. [56a]. explained that poly-l-lysine-functionalized graphene is water soluble and biocompatible was explained by Shan et al. [62]. Conjugated graphene sheets with active amino groups of poly-l-lysine delivered an appropriate biocompatible environment for supplementary functionalization, for instance attaching bioactive molecules. dsDNA can muddle to GO resulting complexes (dsDNA/GO) in the presence of certain salts, which shields dsDNA from being enzymatically digested, and a non-ionic surfactant, such as triton X-100, can block the development of dsDNA/GO complexes, to facilitate the enzymatic digestion of dsDNA is restored, it was demonstrated by Lei et al. [63].
Lately, researchers ought to initiate the synthesis of smaller graphene derivatives, every so often referred as graphene quantum dots (GQDs).

These GQDs display inherent fluorescence, and these are also employed for the theranostic tenacities. Synthesized GQDs possessing diverse color emissions and loaded with anticancer drugs such as doxorubicin were stated to possess an image-guided higher therapeutic efficacy of about 55%–90% cell growth inhibition (Supplementary Table. 3). GO-based drug carriers will turn out to be a hot spot of research at the interface of nanotechnology and biomedicine in the near future for the reason that they permit efficient loading, targeted delivery, and controlled release of drugs, and consequently are encouraging for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene constructed nanomaterials for gene delivery
ce:section.ce:para: For effective gene therapy, what is indispensably utmost important is safe and effective delivery of target genes to a specific site [64]. The central motive of gene therapy is generally curing diseases such as cancer by restoring a mutated gene or replacing a diseased one, and it has acknowledged momentous attention in the past two decades with various successful cases. Owing to the biosafety perils of viral vectors, nonetheless, non-viral gene vectors have been comprehensively explored for gene delivery [64a,65]. Owing to the structural versatility graphene-based carriers have been established as admirable contenders for latent vectors of gene delivery.

Electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged DNA and the positively charged vector [65a] defines the packing mechanism of a gene into a non-viral vector. Chemically synthesized cationic polymer Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is a characteristically castoff as a non-viral gene vector, and it has been acknowledged as the gold standard for the valuation of other synthesized vectors [65a,66]. Parenthetically, PEI is the utmost recurrently used polymer, pooled with GO, as a non-viral gene vector. Since its well acknowledged, graphene materials are not inherently cationic, which is required for gene packing [67]. Quite a lot of groups ensured the synthesis GO-PEI composites [67a,68] or dual polymer functionalized GO, i.e., GO-PEG-PEI [69] and GO-PEI-PEG [67c,70] by way of covalently conjugating PEI with GO through amide linkage [67a,67b,67d,67f]. GO-PEI composite has substantial capability for DNA condensation with ominously lesser cytotoxicity than that of PEI was established by Zhang et al. The in vitro gene transfection of GO-PEI was assessed qualitatively and quantitatively by means of pEGFP and pGL-3, correspondingly, as the reporter genes and displayed analogous gene transfection capability with that of PEI [68c]. The reduced GO competently interacted with ssRNA via π-π interaction and was delivered into HeLa cells was established. The key forces responsible for the packing mechanism are the π-π stacking interactions between the ssRNA bases and the sp2 domains of the graphene sheet as revealed by Molecular dynamics simulations [71]. GO-PEI composite scheme capable of nuclear confined gene delivery by integrating a nuclear targeting sequence (NTS) onto the GO-PEI composite via electrostatic interaction was developed by Dong et al. [72]. Gene delivery efficiency of both HeLa and 293 T cells was ominously enhanced by integration of the NTS compared with those without NTS modification. Genetic payload labelled fluorescently was apparently positioned at the nuclear periphery, which was evidently observed with confocal laser scanning microscopy [67a] (Fig. 10 ). Gene co-delivery and chemotherapeutic drugs attained engrossed substantial interest in cancer therapy for hampering cancer from multifaceted pathways, leading to a predictable improvement in prognosis [73]. Graphene kinfolk's nanomaterials are superlative platforms for this tenacity, owing to their unique physiochemical properties and high accommodation of both genes and conventional anticancer drugs. An evidence-of-idea for drug and gene codelivery was described by Li et al. in the preparation of a chitosan-grafted graphene oxide, comprising of nearly 64 wt% chitosan. Different mechanisms where employed to encapsulate CPT and pDNA into a composite. Despite the fact CPT was loaded by means of π-π interaction, pDNA was loaded by the electrostatic interaction of cationic chitosan and anionic pDNA [74]. Chitosan-conjugated GO was competent to bargain P-gp expression levels in doxorubicin chloride (DOX)-resistant MCF-7 human breast cancer cells by delivering MDR1 siRNA for boosted DOX sensitivity was stated by Chen et al. [75]. The utmost encouraging tactics for the treatment of cancer have been the combination of chemotherapy and gene therapy. One of the supremely effective techniques for assessing antitumor therapy is noninvasive MRI with superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPIO) as a contrast agent. Wang al. lately stated multifunctional chitosan magnetic graphene (CMG) nanoparticles as a theranostic platform for tumor-targeted co-delivery of both drug and gene, despite the fact that concurrently facilitating magnetic resonance imaging [76]. Photothermal effect of graphene-based materials can augment the competence of gene delivery has been established lately. By covalently conjugating GO with PEG and PEI (GO-PEG-PEI) [68b] it was accomplished. Since photothermal heating elevates the cell membrane permeability and leads to amended uptake of plasmid DNA or siRNA cargoes into cells with improved biological effectiveness is the mechanism that was anticipated. By means of depositing gold nanoparticles onto the graphene planar structure the photothermal effect of graphene materials can also be realized. Consequent to the laser irradiation (808 nm), photothermal heating displayed raised temperature in the samples with gold nanoparticles. Moreover, the composite can proficiently load siRNA and transport it into HL-60 cells and down-control the anti-apoptotic Bcl-2, which is auspicious for gene therapy [70].

Majority of the up-to-date studies on graphene-based gene delivery emphasise on the vitro experiments, on the other hand only a few focus on in vivo experiments. For the cure of mouse malignant melanoma Zhang et al., established the GO-PEI composite as the plasmid-based Stat3-specific small interfering RNA (siRNA) carrier [67c]. Stat3 is symbolised as the signal transducer and activator of transcription 3 (Stat3), whose paramount activation stimulates survival in a wide spectrum of human cancers. The in vivo results showed momentous embargo of tumor growth consequent to the delivery of plasmid-based Stat3 siRNA by the GO-PEI composite. Subsequent histological examination and blood chemistry analysis detected no noteworthy side effects. The GO-PEI composite was also realized to be a talented gene vector for zebrafish embryos [67f] (Fig. 11 ). Since this study, the authors validated 90% transfection in zebrafish embryos by a GO-PEI composite transporting pEGFP, with high feasibility, paralleled to efficiencies of 30% or lesser for recognized transfection technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene constructed nanomaterials for photosensitizer delivery
ce:section.ce:para: As a talented modality to treat a wide range of cancers in the clinics photosensitizers (PSs) render a vital role in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [77]. PDT renders a superlative benefit of high selectivity and minimal invasiveness paralleled to other conventional cancer therapeutic ways [78]. The principal mechanism of PDT might be recognized as a uninterrupted physiochemical process as well as selective activation of the PS upon irradiation by a nontoxic light (generally NIR light), the PS-activated transfer of energy to molecular oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species for instance singlet oxygen, and the subsequent dysfunction of the biological molecules by oxidation of reactive oxygen species.184 Through entirely exposing the diseased tissue to external light throughout the course of therapy the destruction of normal tissue can be mostly avoided. The meagre accumulation of PS within the tumor site at both tissue and cellular levels as well as the hydrophobic nature of most of PSs, which can be solved by the usage of nanocarriers in the PDT [79]. Since graphene-based nanomaterials possess characteristic π-π network structure which is analogous to that of majority of the PSs, they can function as brilliant PS carriers. Dong et al. synthesized a PEG-conjugated GO for the delivery of zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc) as a PS carrier [80]. ZnPc was proficiently loaded in NGO-PEG via π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, ensuring a drug loading efficiency up to 14 wt%. This is considerably more than conventional liposomal or polymeric carriers [81]. Through the usage of the MCF-7 cell line as an in vitro model, the ZnPc-loaded GO was detected to be proficiently engulfed intracellularly, with elevated photocytotoxicity on subsequent irradiation by Xe light filtered by a band-path filter of 400 nm. Almost immediately later, substantial exertions have been dedicated to photodynamic therapy constructed on the graphene-based nanomaterials [82].

Numerous PSs have been testified on graphene materials comprising ZnPc [80], chlorin e683, hypocrellin A (HA) [82b], hypocrellin B (HB) [84], and methylene blue [85]. As a second generation photosensitizer Chlorin e6 (Ce6), remained most frequently employed PS owing to its high absorption in the red spectral region, high accessibility, high photochemical efficiency, hydrophilic properties, and easy functionalization (e.g., with carboxyl groups). Nano-sized GO covalently linked with Ce6 via a redox-responsive cleavable disulfide linkage was synthesized by Choi et al. The Ce6-conjugated GO stood non-fluorescent and nonphototoxic, even after subsequent light irradiation. In disparity, it turned out to be highly fluorescent and phototoxic inside cancer cells upon irradiation. It was verified that intracellular glutathione triggered the reactivation of the photochemical property due detachment of Ce6 from the GO after disulfide bond cleavage [86]. After the amalgamation of chlorin Ce6 into GO via a degradable polymer (hyaluronic acid, HA), a hyaluronidase-activatable GO-HA-Ce6 composite for photodynamic therapy was synthesized later on by the same group. Devoid of HA degradation, Ce6 displayed minimal fluorescence, displaying its extraordinary juxtaposition with the GO structure. On the other hand, subsequent to the exposure to HAdase, the fluorescence was recovered. The photochemical effect was evidently sustained by exposure of the composite to the A549 human lung cancer cells overexpressing lysosomal HAdase. Compared to both free Ce6 and the HA-Ce6 conjugate a remarkably improved efficacy was observed [83c].

It was observed that the photothermal effect of nano-graphene oxide can be employed not only for augmenting its cellular uptake but correspondingly for the combined photothermal and photodynamic therapy by Tae et al. [85a]. Over-all ablation of the tumor tissue was accomplished after intravenous injection of the complex into tumor-bearing mice and following exposure to NIR lights. This work displayed the latent of GO for synergistic phototherapy of tumors in vivo (Fig. 12 ). The combined chemotherapy and photodynamic therapy was also lately described by way of loading both DOX and Ce6 on GO82d. Merging DOX with Ce6 at a molar ratio of 1:2 resulted in the highest synergism was displayed by the reduction indexes.

Lately it was established that the graphene quantum dots viable photodynamic agents [87].25 Owing to the presence of carbon free radicals at the GQDs surface which was revealed by an electron paramagnetic resonance analysis that revealed a narrow derivative signal of Lorentzian shape centred at g = 2.0051. Subsequent irradiation with blue light, the electron paramagnetic resonance signal intensified, viewing the capability of GQDs to breed singlet oxygen upon photoexcitation. The results were shown to be in accordance with the assay of the singlet oxygen sensor. GQDs killed the U251 human glioma cells by breeding of singlet oxygen, which instigated oxidative stress was revealed by the in vitro studies. Morphological and biochemical characteristics of both apoptosis and autophagy were indicated by the cell death caused by the GQDs. In addition, GO is able to generate reactive oxygen species after visible light irradiation of inorganic nanoparticles for instance TiO2 and ZnO deposited on the GO surface [88], displaying potential in photodynamic therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene constructed nanomaterials for theranostic applications
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene Constructed nanomaterials are exceedingly flexible owing to their intrinsically distinctive chemical and physical properties [76,89]. Due to their enormous surface area and planar structure GO bid's ample accommodation for different bio-agents. Conjugation of different bio-agents can be realized by utilizing the abundance of functional groups as well as the π-π network (Fig. 13 ). For multifunctional biomedical uses, besides therapeutic drugs, genes, and supplementary biomolecules, numerous imaging agents can also be encapsulated into graphene-based materials [90].

Liu et al. synthesized a PEG-conjugated Fe3O4-GO hybrid scheme that facilitated magnetically targeted drug delivery for cancer theranostic [91]. The hybrid scheme unveiled robust optical absorbance commencing at visible to the near-infrared (NIR) region and may perhaps be exploited for localized photothermal ablation of cancer cells, directed by the magnetic field. Additionally, in vivo magnetic resonance (MR) imaging of tumor-bearing mice was realized through usage of the hybrid system as T2 contrast agent. For the concurrent delivery the gene, drugs, genes, and MRI contrast agents packing capability was combined with the hybrid Fe3O4-GO system [92]. Cationic chitosan was amalgamated into the Fe3O4-GO hybrid, permitting condensed DNA by means of electrostatic interaction. Chitosan-modified Fe3O4-GO hybrid competently condensed DNA at a weight ratio as low as 1:1 was disclosed by the binding experiment. Co-localization of the anticancer drug DOX and expressed GFP were revealed by the In vivo studies [93]. Dong et al. lately examined the photothermal effect of a Pluronic F127-functionalized Fe3O4-GO hybrid for in vitro chemophototherapy. Upon irradiation with a 660 nm laser of 0.5 W/cm2, the temperature of the aqueous hybrid solution (5 mg/mL) was expressively amplified. Cytotoxicity of the GO/Fe3O4/PF127 nanohybrid, loaded with DOX, was extraordinarily improved contrary to HeLa cells upon photoirradiation [94] (Fig. 12c). A “theranostic” graphene-based nanoplatform by integrating an MRI contrast agent, gadolinium, into the graphene structure through a modest physical sorption process was established by Dong et al. A loading efficiency of up to 0.314 mg/mg of Gd3+was realized [95]. The transitional product was extra reformed with folic acid (FA), to advance its tumor targeting effectiveness for imaging and treatment. The MRI results displayed that the FA-conjugated GO-PEG showed grander tumor-targeted imaging effectiveness over free Gd3+. The DOX-loaded complexes unveiled noticeable cytotoxic effects on consequent HepG2 cancer cells.

For biological application before using nanoparticles with in vitro and in vivo organelles it is very important to examine their physicochemical interaction. For the reason that graphene has been predominantly deliberated as an electronic material, several studies have recognized graphene for bioapplications for instance tissue engineering, drug delivery, and stem cell research. Nevertheless, wide-ranging observations are prerequisite in both in vivo and in vitro to scrutinize their cell and bio molecular interactions. Graphene constituted up of only carbon atoms; nevertheless, GO and graphene quantum dots possess oxygen because of the oxidation. Although limited in vitro and in vivo toxicology studies have been testified hitherto, there has not been ample emphasis on biochemistry and histological impact [99]. The in vivo studies employing GO are established on the evaluation of bioaccumulation and excretion. In the occasion of the toxicity of nanomaterials the route of administration is also one of the vital parameters to be well-thought-out. There is a necessity for further comprehensive and precise in vitro and in vivo studies concerning the toxicity of the GO owing to the growing significance of GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro toxicity of graphene constructed nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular internalization and in vitro cytotoxicity of functionalized graphene derivatives was investigated over a couple of years by numerous research teams: GOs and GQDs by making use of different categories of mammalian cells. Majority of these studies usually established low cytotoxicity and reasonably high cellular uptake, which rendered graphene-based materials appropriate for a number of biomedical applications [100]. On the other hand, several researchers claimed that the cytotoxicity of a few hundred nanometre or micro-meter sized GOs is considerably higher compared that of GQDs, which must not be overlooked in biomedical applications [99b,100c]. Especially, certain studies signposted that GOs and GO-based nanoplatelets are associated with austere cytotoxicity and lung diseases [101]. Heterogeneous cell-specific cytotoxicity of GOs by performing cytotoxicity screening of GOs on multiple different cell lines was revealed some researchers [102].

On the whole, the cytotoxicity of graphene constructed materials was established to be firmly linked to the size of particles, which may perhaps to some extent explain lower cytotoxicity of a few nano-meter sized GQDs compared to a few micro-meter sized GOs [100c,101b,103]. Quite the opposite, Cytotoxicity of graphene-based materials is independent of size was contended over and over again by Akhavan et al. the core mechanism of the observed cytotoxicity is however direct interaction of the sharp edges of graphene with the cell membranes [104]. It is to be believed that nanosized GOs can also be lethal to mammalian cells as per researchers. As a consequence, comprehensive toxicity mechanisms concerning to the size and shape of graphene constructed materials are yet ambiguous and further studies appear obligatory.

Furthermore, graphene functionalization effects in cell membrane permeability and cytotoxicity were deliberate by various researchers. By covalently attaching hydrophilic molecules for instance polyethylene glycol (PEG) to the edges of graphene boosts solubility and biocompatibility in a biological environment was normally deliberated by the researchers [56a]. A number of researchers examined potential toxicity effects of the functional groups by amending them with COOH, NH2, CO–N(CH3)2, and –PEG [56a,105]. No discrete toxicity fluctuations amongst these GQD modifications were shown by quantitative data analysis, despite the fact that the cell membrane permeability amplified correspondingly in the order of –PEG, OH, and NH2 [106]. Researchers who are undertaking to engage modified graphene derivatives since all of them displayed precisely low cytotoxicity these results are encouraging [107]. Distinctive manners amongst pristine/hydrophobic graphene and carboxylated/hydrophilic graphene in biological environments were deliberated by researcher's Sasidharan et al., in 2011. In comparison to the pristine graphene, carboxylated graphene appease hydrophobic interaction with the cell membrane and the allied toxic effects for instance the deformation of cell membrane and increased intracellular ROS level and ensuing apoptosis [108]. Without a doubt, graphene functionalization renders a vibrant role not only in cell-nanoparticle interactions, nevertheless also in enzyme-catalyzed biodegradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicity of graphene constructed nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the in vitro toxicity studies discussed above, several researchers have discovered in vivo bio-distribution and toxicology of graphene based materials lately. Enduring in vivo pharmacokinetics and bio distribution of PEGylated I-labeled nanographene sheets (NGS) with systemic toxicology investigation where deliberated by Yang et al., in 2010.75 The I-NGS-PEG radioactivity levels were evaluated in the blood and several distinctive organs repeatedly subsequent to intravenous injection. Generally, they established insistently diminished radioactivity levels of I-NGS-PEG in most organs. Researchers assumed that small NGS-PEG particles may perhaps be emptied out by renal or faecal excretion. After carrying out blood biochemistry and hematology analysis the researchers also investigated long term in vivo toxicology for over 3 month. Mice injected with 20 mg kg−1 NGS-PEG were sacrificed at different time spans, and all factors from the blood biochemistry and haematological data did not point out any substantial toxicity. The in vivo bio distribution and toxicology studies of functionalized nano-GOs by administering it via two other main paths: oral feeding and intraperitoneal (i.p.) injection 7 studies were carried out by the same research group in 2013. Researchers discovered that oral administration brought no noticeable tissue uptake, whereas i. p. injection commanded to high build-up of nano-GOs in the RES system for a longer duration. Researcher's established that both routes did not produce substantial toxicity to the treated animals, considering the outcomes achieved through the i. p. injection.

The in vivo biodistribution and toxicity of carboxylated GQDs by intravenously injecting them into mice was testified in 2014 by Nurunnabi et al.76 The build-up and impending toxicity were tested by carrying out a long-term serum biochemical analysis and histological estimations. Inclusively, the study showed no grave in vivo toxicity and GQDs were chiefly detected in the liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor sites (Fig. 14 ). Additional studies established that GQDs did not produce any substantial organ damage or abrasions in mice that were treated with GQDs by administering 5 mg kg−1 or 10 mg kg−1 dosages for 21 days. These results were shadowed by analogous assumptions of in vivo bio-distribution findings, exemplifying rapid clearance of GQDs from kidneys devoid of substantial build-up in the main organs.98These biocompatibility finding results recommend that GQDs may perhaps be castoff in clinical solicitations in the near future. On the other hand, these results also point out that high doses of GOs may perhaps be toxic and have a disastrous consequence, on good agreement with the results of in vitro studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety, biodegradation and other applications of graphene constructed nanomaterials

ce:section.ce:para: Before realising the vision of employing the graphene-based nanomaterials for clinical application their safety and toxicity must be investigated. The toxicity of these graphene based materials has been found to be exceedingly reliant on their physiochemical parameters comprising lateral size, layer number, surface charge, oxidative stress, hydrophobicity, surface functionalization, and dosage [49]. Elements like these render a shared role at the nano-bio interfaces, for that reason defining the behaviours of the carrier systems at the bimolecular, cellular, and tissue levels [49,85b,94,102,110]. Owing their hydrophobic properties as well as inherent high surface area which lead them to absorb folic acid and deplete the cells normal levels and lead to cell growth inhibition [110c]. Toxicological effects on normal human lung cells in terms of cytotoxicity and apoptosis was deliberated by Vallabani et al. A substantial concentration and time-dependent decline in cell viability was witnessed at varied concentrations (10–100 g/mL), and a momentous upsurge of early and late apoptotic cells was witnessed matched to control cells [111]. In case of halogenated graphene, the cytotoxicity in contrast to human lung carcinoma epithelial cells (A549) was found to be in the order of GO-Cl > GO-Br > GO-I [112]. Graphene quantum dots categorized with smaller size displayed lower cytotoxicity compared to those of micrometre-sized GO in terms of cell viability, internal cellular ROS level, mutilation to mitochondrial membrane potential, and cell cycle [113]. The lower cytotoxicity of GQDs signposted that they may perhaps be engaged as vehicles, for targeted drug delivery.

Like former quite a lot of factors of in vitro and in vivo toxicity researches are significant, comprehensive insight into the oxidation biodegradation processes of graphene-based materials is crucial for widespread and constructive applications. By incubating every single solution with low concentrations of hydrogen peroxide and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) enzyme-catalyzed oxidation of GOs and rGO [114] was by Kotchey et al., in 2011. The degree of functionalization is candidly linked with the degree of enzyme-catalyzed oxidation was remarkably revealed by this study. Mild enzymatic oxidation with HRP prompted the creation of holey graphene oxide, which in due course resulted in fully oxidized debris of GO flakes was established by the results of Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. 15 ). Alternatively, rGOs incubation with HRP did not yield any noteworthy changes in terms of enzymatic oxidation.

The authors inferred that the manifestation of functional groups on GOs prompted weaker binding with HRP and permitted the enzyme to be additionally vibrant; the catalytic heme site of HRP was as a consequence brought in the vicinity of GOs. Conversely, further hydrophobic rGOs fashioned snugger binding with HRP devoid of making contacts with the catalytic heme site. The same research group in 2012, stated comprehensive exploration on the enzyme-catalyzed degradation of CNMs by means of HRP and myeloperoxidase (MPO) [116]. The study substantiated encouraging traits of functionalized CNMs for in vivo applications as they are most probably biodegradable by intracellular enzymes with peroxidase activity such as human MPO (hMPO). On the other hand, it should be remarked that the experimental results are not straight away interrelated with real biodegradation in the human body as the physiological hMPO levels are by and large more diluted than the experimental conditions. Furthermore, the oxidative debris of GO nanoflakes may perhaps be an additional conceivable source of toxicity.

Fan et al., [117] formulated macroscopic free standing GO and rGO paper from their suspension by vacuum filtration technique, and bring into being that these papers demonstrate robust antibacterial effect. Bearing in mind the proportions and low cost of the graphene-based antibacterial paper, this study unwraps innovative opportunities for the usage of GO in environmental and clinical purposes. Subsequently Akhavan et al. [118], studied antibacterial impacts of graphene nanosheets in the shape of nanowalls consigned on stainless steel substrates for both Gram-positive and Gram-negative models of bacteria. It is attention-grabbing to note that the Gram-negative Esch-erichia coli bacteria with an outer membrane were additionally resistant to the cell membrane damage induced by the nanowalls compared to that of the Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus devoid of the outer membrane. Additionally, the rGO nanowalls stood extra toxic to the bacteria compared to the GO nanowalls. Charge transfer between the bacteria and the extra polished edges of the rGO nano-walls during the contact interaction results into better antibacterial activity of the rGO nanowalls as per researchers.

Liu et al. [119], additionally investigated the procedure of antibacterial effect of 4 kinds of graphene derivatives, graphite (Gt), graphite oxide (GtO), GO, and rGO. Authors established that the antibacterial actions decline in the order of GO, rGO, Gt, and GtO. Authors ascribed the antibacterial effect too mutually to the membrane and oxidative stress, and subsequently suggested a three-step mechanism for the antibacterial effect, analogous to that of carbon nanotube. This study is imperative for us to comprehend the GO-bacteria dealings and to steer development of improved graphene-based antibacterial materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene constructed scaffold for cell culture
ce:section.ce:para: The conduct of NIH-3T3 fibroblasts as a prototypical of mammalian cells budding on a supported film of GO was studied by Min group [120]. Their work advocates that GO film tempts no considerably harmful effect on the mammalian cells pertaining to adhesion, and displays extraordinarily high gene transfection efficiency, 250% that of cells grown on a cover glass substrate. This outcome signposted the latent use of GO as a surface coating material for implant.

A graphene/chitosan film formed by means of solution casting technique has also stood examined as scaffold materials in tissue engineering [121]. The study point out that the graphene-based film does not obstruct the multiplying of human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs). As an alternative, it hastens their precise differentiation into bone cells in a well-ordered fashion via use of growth factors and osteogenic inducers, proposing the impending application for proliferation and transplantation of stem cells and their precise differentiation into muscles, bones, and cartilages for bone regeneration therapy [122](Fig. 16 ). An additional research [123]discovered that chemical vapor deposition (CVD) grown graphene substrate is biocompatible for human osteoblasts in addition to the hMSCs with advanced proliferation of cell, in comparison to the SiO2 substrate, and stimulates the growth and differentiation of cells.

Encouraged by the studies stated above, Tang and Cheng et al. [125]examined CVD-grown graphene film as a substrate of neurites, one of the crucial structures for neural operations in the course of the development in a mouse hippocampal culture model. Their results specify that the neurite numbers average length on graphene film were considerably boosted in the course of 2–7 days subsequent to the cell seeding equated with tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) substrates. Additionally, GAP-43 manifestation was momentously enriched in graphene group compared to TCPS group, because of likely enhancement of neurite sprouting and outgrowth. This research proposes the impending application of pristine graphene as an innovative material for neural interfacing.
The thought-provoking hitherto preliminary work pronounced above advocates the probable applications of graphene as antibacterial materials and scaffold materials for cell culture.

Graphene constructed nanomaterials have stood revealed experimentally, in vitro and in vivo, to possess pronounced potential in biomedical applications comprising biomedical, drug delivery, bio sensing, stem cell technology, and photothermal therapy. Moreover, numerous types of graphene also enjoy other amazing properties counting high Raman scattering intensity, large absorption cross section in the NIR region, sharp photoacoustic contrast with the NIR incident beam, which all are indispensable properties for bio-imaging. The progresses achieved in this area hitherto are thrilling and encouraging, the challenges we face, on the other hand, are also very gigantic and must be overcome. One of such challenges is detailed and insightful understanding of graphene-cell (or tissue, organ) interactions, specifically the cellular uptake mechanism. Such understanding undoubtedly eases development of additional competent graphene or GO-based nanoplatform for drug bioimaging, delivery, bio sensing and other applications, which, however, is yet lacking at the moment. The toxicity of graphene and GO, at in vitro and in vivo level, is additional most important apprehension because up-to-date there are only a few publications in this respect [126]. The earliest results specify that the physicochemical properties for instance flat shape and charges, are meticulously connected to the cytotoxicity, and affect in vivo biodistribution and fate of GO [127]. The approach of the in vitro biotoxicity instigated by graphene is associated to oxidative stress and damage of cell membrane. Obviously, a methodical research is extremely looked-for to sort out the safety apprehensions prior to the practical applications of graphene in biomedicine.

So far as the GO based bioimaging is concerned, even if the GQDs have distinctive gains over II-IV QDs in their inherent biocompatibility, safety and facile functionalization, the weak fluorescence intensity (with quantum yield about 10%) and broad emission band (with bandwidth be-yond 100 nm) are undoubtedly foremost hindrances for their applications in biodetection and labelling. Comprehensive research is prerequisite to augment the reaction conditions with proper analytical methods to obtain an integrated structure of GQDs with improved properties. Waste management and utilization of by-products are the principal tasks which concerns to the large scale production of GQDs by chemical exfoliation methods. Since the synthesis process is conducted in highly acidic media, the acids, such as nitric acid and sulfuric acid, are prerequisite to be neutralized by adding additional amount of salts [50a]. Consequently, it yields a mammoth quantity of by-products, which is one of the chief concerns for scaling-up the production system. Additional sophisticated toxicity studies are prerequisite to detect the bio-degradation of GQDs in biological systems after administration. Research is also mandatory to evaluate if the graphene constituents interact with genetic molecules such as DNA. GQDs necessitate additional long term biosafety studies prior to be considered as biomaterials for biological applications. Therefore additional efforts need to be paid to prepare GQDs with a good control of size and size distribution, the surface defects, and their functionalization, to advance fluorescence quantum yield and other important properties.

The up-to-date research on delivery of drugs, genes, we anticipate, will spread out to that of proteins, growth factors, and other biomolecules, either single or combined use of the abovementioned biomolecules, for cancer and other disease therapies. Given its structural topographies and unique physicochemical properties, design and construction of GO-based theranostic platform with multifunctionalities and multimodalities is an innovative way to hunt. Certainly, these aims can only be touched by the cooperative determinations from chemistry, biomedicine, materials sciences, and nanotechnology. To this end, development of appropriate chemical synthesis and functionalization tactics for accurate control over size, size distribution, morphology, structural defects, and oxygen-containing groups of GO is immediately needed, as this is meticulously interrelated to the performance of the GO-based nanomaterials for biomedical applications, and the safety issues as well.

The studies on graphene/GO constructed scaffold materials for cell culture are fairly fresh innovative route that warrants special courtesy. The research in this area up to now established that graphene and GO are able to hasten the growth, differentiation, and proliferation of stem cells, and hence possess prodigious potential in tissue engineering, regenerative medicine, and other biomedical fields.

We have selectively reviewed the remarkably hasty and thrilling advances in the biomedical applications of graphene with focus on bioimaging and drug delivery, archived in the last few years. This inclination, for sure will endure and even get faster in the years to come.

I hereby declare that this work is solely a theoretical review work complied from last few years of research on graphene. I have tried to keep this work free from plagiarism and other kinds of monopolies to the best of my knowledge.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
